metric: 1-NN leave-one-out species accuracy on DINOv2 embeddings (cosine) vs geography (haversine)
conclusion: Richness is the strongest single predictor of embedding benefit (rho=+0.79). The embedding-minus-geographic separability GAP is a usable, interpretable correlate (rho=+0.69; geo_sep alone rho=-0.52 -- the embedding helps less when geography already separates species), but it is confounded with richness and does NOT cleanly add an independent signal. Neither embedding separability, geographic separability, nor their gap explains the Arachnida anomaly: Arachnida has a normal positive emb-geo gap (+0.26) yet a negative benefit (-1.67). Two separability-based mechanism hypotheses for Arachnida have now been tested and refuted; the anomaly is open.
bioclip_capstone: BioCLIP raises 1-NN species separability for EVERY taxon vs DINOv2, and most for the arthropod taxa generic embeddings failed (Arachnida +0.168: 0.453->0.622; Insecta +0.237). This is the mechanism behind the Arachnida rescue (benefit -1.67 -> +19.78): domain-match raises separability where it was deficient. Cross-taxon, raw separability still does not linearly predict benefit (richness confound: separability falls as richness rises while benefit rises) -- the benefit MAGNITUDE is richness-driven, the SIGN/rescue is domain-match.
bioclip_vs_geo: For BioCLIP, embedding 1-NN species separability exceeds geographic separability for ALL 8 taxa (margin +0.25 to +0.56), consistent with BioCLIP winning on every taxon — the domain-match manifests as out-separating geography, including the arthropods generic embeddings could not. Honest caveat: the same emb>geo comparison was UNRELIABLE for generic DINOv2 (Arachnida had DINOv2 emb>geo yet lost), so this is descriptive consistency for a domain-matched embedding, not a validated universal predictor.
